g.data-narration: Welcome to Events and Process States - the foundation of operating system process management.
A process is a program in execution - it's not just code but includes program counter, registers, stack, and resources.
g.text: A process is a program in execution with its own
execution context: PC, registers, stack, data section.

g.data-narration: Modern OS use multiprogramming to run multiple processes, creating the illusion of simultaneous execution on one CPU.
g.text: Multiprogramming keeps multiple processes in memory,

g.data-narration: The Process Control Block (PCB) is the brain of each process, storing state, PC, registers, memory info, and I/O status.
g.text: Save running process state to its PCB,
load new process state from its PCB.

g.data-narration: The five fundamental process states are: New (being created), Ready (waiting for CPU), Running (on CPU), Blocked (waiting for I/O), and Terminated.
g.text: Only ONE process can be RUNNING on a single-processor system

g.data-narration: Seven transitions connect these states: New to Ready (admission), Ready to Running (dispatch), Running to Ready (preemption), Running to Blocked (I/O request), and more.
g.text: • Running→Ready: Time slice expires OR higher priority process arrives
• Running→Blocked: I/O request or resource wait
• Blocked→Ready: I/O complete or resource available

g.data-narration: Context switching saves the running process's state to PCB and loads another process's state. This overhead can take 1-1000 microseconds.
Suspended states handle memory pressure: Ready Suspended and Blocked Suspended processes are swapped to disk, not in memory.
OS maintains three types of queues: Job Queue (all processes), Ready Queue (CPU-bound), and Device Queues (I/O bound).
Example: A word processor loading a large document moves through states: New→Ready→Running→Blocked (I/O)→Ready→Running.
In round-robin scheduling with 4ms time quantum, processes cycle through Ready and Running states every time slice.
Key points: Only one running process per CPU, PCB stores all process info, context switching has overhead, and suspended states handle memory pressure.
g.text: 1. Only ONE process can be RUNNING on a single-processor system at any instant
2. PCB is the brain - stores complete process state for context switching
3. Context switch overhead: 1-1000μs of pure CPU cost
4. Suspended states = swapped to disk (different from blocked!)

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Memorize five states, know what causes each transition, understand PCB components, and practice drawing state diagrams.
g.text: • Remember: New → Ready (admission) • Ready → Running (dispatch) • Running → Blocked (I/O) • Running → Ready (preempt)
• Know 5+ PCB components • Difference between blocked and suspended • Preemptive vs non-preemptive scheduling
